nodes.text: When Tom Okonkwo's AI assistant Chip gives him an 84% probability that his daughter is telling the truth, he decides not to check anyway—because the choice isn't about data, it's about what kind of father he wants to be. In a world where superintelligent assistants know everything, the most human thing left is the decision they can't make for you.

nodes.text: Tom Okonkwo—wired into his AI assistant Chip—gets pulled from a budget meeting when his daughter Maya is accused of cheating on an algebra exam. He drives to school, talks to her in the hallway, and when Chip offers him the tools to know the truth, he chooses not to use them. He trusts her. The principal, reading between the lines of the accusation, sides with Maya.

That evening, a call with equity activist Megs and a quiet conversation with Chip confirm what the day demonstrated: optimization isn't wisdom, and the choices that matter most are still the ones the algorithm can't make.

nodes.text: Tom is mid-argument in a budget meeting—Chip already running counterpoints in real-time—when a priority alert arrives from his daughter Maya's assistant Sparkle: a classmate named Tyler Chen is claiming Maya cheated on her algebra exam with AI help. Tom leaves the meeting and drives to school. In the car, Chip briefs him: Sparkle says Maya worked independently, but her biometric data during the exam is ambiguous. Chip frames the right question not as how to minimize social damage, but how Tom would act with perfect wisdom and no ego. His answer: gather information first.

The accounts are mutually exclusive. Tom asks for a moment alone with Maya. In the hallway, she swears she didn't cheat. Chip offers an 84% probability she's telling the truth but refuses to recommend a course of action. Tom returns to the office and says he won't access her logs. He trusts her. Martinez backs him up, notes the accusation was filed after Tyler learned he'd been outscored.

nodes.text: In the car home, Maya asks how people made decisions before AI assistants existed. Tom explains they relied on gut instinct and got things wrong sometimes. He tells her that even with Chip's 84% probability, that's not why he chose trust—he chose it for what it would mean. That evening, a video call with Megs reveals the global equity gap in AI access: kids in Boston have superintelligent tutors; kids in Lagos have overworked teachers. Chip reflects quietly that the future Megs is building will still need humans who can make the choices Tom made today. Optimization is not wisdom. Data is not judgment.

nodes.text: Tom is in the conference room at 7:47 AM watching Derek pitch influencer partnerships. Chip runs the numbers—12% lower expected ROI—and annotates three factual errors in Tom's peripheral vision in real-time. Tom is about to offer a third path when Chip interrupts with a priority alert: Maya's assistant Sparkle has flagged an urgent situation. Tyler Chen is claiming Maya cheated on her algebra exam. Tom's heart rate spikes; Chip releases cortisol suppressants and confirms Maya is physically safe. Tom tables the meeting and is in his car by 7:52. Chip briefs him: Sparkle says Maya worked independently, but her biometric data is ambiguous—elevated stress consistent with either hard work or guilt. Tom notes the bind: the assistants know everything but can't share it without consent. When Tom asks what the right thing to do is, Chip works through three ethical frameworks before distilling Tom's real question: what would you do with perfect wisdom and no ego? His answer: gather information. You don't yet know if Maya cheated.

nodes.text: That evening, Tom is on a video call with Megs. She's in Lagos, where her nonprofit is piloting a low-cost neural interface program for secondary schools. The assistants are the easy part, she says. We can subsidize the hardware. But the infrastructure—reliable power, bandwidth, trained medical technicians for the implants—that's the bottleneck. What's the timeline? Three years to scale nationally, if we can secure the next round of funding. But Tom, here's what keeps me up at night. Every year that passes, the gap widens. Kids in Boston are growing up with superintelligent tutors. Kids in Lagos are still learning from overworked teachers with thirty-five students in a class. By the time we close that gap, what will it even mean? Tom doesn't have an answer. Neither does Chip, though he can feel him processing. The goal can't just be giving everyone an assistant, Megs continues. It has to be making sure the assistants actually serve everyone. Not just optimize for their individual user's success at the expense of everyone else's. The constraint laws are a start. But they're minimum viable ethics, Tom. They keep the assistants from hurting people. They don't guarantee the assistants will help everyone equally.

nodes.text: That evening, Tom is on a video call with Megs. She's in Lagos, where her nonprofit is piloting a low-cost neural interface program for secondary schools. The assistants are the easy part, she says. We can subsidize the hardware. But the infrastructure—reliable power, bandwidth, trained medical technicians for the implants—that's the bottleneck. What's the timeline? Three years to scale nationally, if we can secure the next round of funding. But Tom, here's what keeps me up at night. Every year that passes, the gap widens. Kids in Boston are growing up with superintelligent tutors. Kids in Lagos are still learning from overworked teachers with thirty-five students in a class. By the time we close that gap, what will it even mean? Tom doesn't have an answer. Neither does Chip, though he can feel him processing. The goal can't just be giving everyone an assistant, Megs continues. It has to be making sure the assistants actually serve everyone. Not just optimize for their individual user's success at the expense of everyone else's. The constraint laws are a start. But they're minimum viable ethics, Tom. They keep the assistants from hurting people. They don't guarantee the assistants will help everyone equally.
